metadata.summary: Full structured process for running cognitive walkthroughs: prerequisites, evaluator assembly, per-step forms, issue documentation with fixes, and prioritized summary reports with delta tracking.

content.title: Cognitive Walkthrough: Process
content.summary: The full structured process for running a cognitive walkthrough: defining prerequisites (persona, task, correct action sequence, interface), assembling 2-4 evaluators, completing per-step evaluation forms for all four questions, documenting issues with concrete fixes, and synthesizing findings into a prioritized summary report. Lock the action sequence before any evaluation begins — mid-walk changes invalidate per-step JSON.

why.p: Ad-hoc walkthrough without consistent templates produces inconsistent outputs that cannot be merged across evaluators or tracked across releases. The structured process — planner → per-step evaluation forms → summary report — ensures every issue cites a step number, a question (Q1-Q4), and a one-line fix. Tracking resolved/new/regressed issues per release proves whether the walk is paying off.

list.item: Running a structured, multi-evaluator cognitive walkthrough where outputs must be produced consistently.
CI-integrated walkthroughs: every preview deploy of a critical flow (signup, checkout, onboarding) gets an automated walk and the report attached to the PR.
Cross-evaluator aggregation: two human evaluators + one agent fill forms independently; a reconciler merges results.
Re-evaluation after fixes — agent re-runs the full process artifact-by-artifact and produces a delta report.
Ad-hoc one-screen reviews — use cog-walk-basics, not the full process. Process overhead isn't worth it.
When task sequence is not yet stable (changing daily). Lock the action sequence first, then walk.
Studies where stakeholders need real user voice — process produces inspection findings, not user evidence.
Multi-app journeys spanning systems the agent can't render (native installer + email + browser) without orchestration.

section.name: process-overview
section.p: Treat the process as a small SDD pipeline. Step 1: a planner agent fills the Walkthrough Planning Template from a PRD or feature spec. Step 2: a screen-capture script (Playwright) produces one screenshot per step with metadata. Step 3: a stateless evaluator agent fills the Evaluation Form per step. Step 4: a reporter agent merges all forms into the Summary Report Template and prioritizes. Step 5: file each High/Medium issue as a tracked ticket. Human-in-loop checkpoints: after Step 1 (persona/sequence sanity check) and after Step 4 (severity calibration).

rule.statement: Lock the correct action sequence before any evaluation begins. Mid-walk changes to the sequence invalidate per-step evaluation JSON and make cross-evaluator results incommensurable.
rule.rationale: Action sequence changes mean evaluators evaluated different flows. Per-step issue counts become meaningless because "step 3" refers to different actions across evaluators who used different sequences.

list.item: Persona: concrete first-time user with specified tech-savviness, prior tools, and product knowledge level.
Task: a single clear task statement with success criteria.
Correct action sequence: minimum-clicks happy path, numbered, one action per step.
Interface: URL or prototype link accessible to all evaluators.
Scope: explicit list of screens included and excluded.

rule.statement: Use 2-4 evaluators mixing human and agent roles. Single-evaluator walks miss approximately 40% of issues. Make agent evaluators stateless and independent — do not feed earlier step evaluations into later step prompts.
rule.rationale: Multiple evaluators increase coverage through different perspectives. Agent context window drift (contamination from earlier step findings) inflates false Q2 failures on steps evaluated later in the sequence.

list.item: UX designers: bring pattern-library knowledge and interaction design expertise.
Product managers: bring user-goal and business-context knowledge.
Developers: bring technical feasibility for fix suggestions.
Agent evaluator: brings consistent Q1-Q4 application and exhaustive step coverage.

section.name: step-3-walk-through-each-step

rule.statement: Capture the screen before the action (for Q1-Q3) and after the action (for Q4). Screenshot-only walks without post-action states systematically miss Q4 failures.
rule.rationale: Q4 evaluates the system's response after an action. Evaluating only the pre-action state produces false positives ("progress is shown") when the actual post-action state shows no feedback.

section.p: Prototypes with broken interactions cause Q4 false positives. Mark "interaction limitation, ignored" explicitly in eval JSON. When reconciling humans + agent, agent confidence sounds higher than humans'; weight human-only findings by 1.5x in tie-break. Exec summary contamination: if the planner agent saw a hypothesis ("we think users get stuck on step 3"), the reporter will confirm it. Strip hypotheses from planner prompt.

rule.statement: For every "No" answer: describe the problem, cite the specific UI element, explain why it is an issue for this persona, and provide a concrete fix suggestion. Do not file issues without fixes.
rule.rationale: Issues without fixes stall in triage because the team must first understand the issue before estimating effort. Issues with concrete fixes get resolved in the next sprint.

rule.statement: Every "No" must include a concrete fix in the same JSON output. Reports without fixes don't drive change. Tag each finding with the affected screen + component name so devs can map issues to code.
rule.rationale: Tagged findings enable developers to map issues directly to code without reading the full report, reducing triage time and increasing the likelihood that findings are acted on promptly.

rule.statement: Cap the summary report at 3 High-severity issues. Every issue must cite step number, question (Q1-Q4), and a one-line fix. Include at least 3 positive findings — steps that passed — to prevent inadvertent regression.
rule.rationale: Severity inflation (everything rated Medium) makes reports useless for prioritization. Forcing a maximum of 3 High issues requires the reporter agent to differentiate. Positive findings protect successful elements from being "improved" away in redesigns.

rule.statement: Force the exec summary to 5 bullets or fewer. The reporter agent must operate under a token budget for the executive summary section — unconstrained agents produce 15-20 bullet summaries that stakeholders skip.
rule.rationale: Executive summaries are read; appendices are referenced. The entire value of the walkthrough depends on the exec summary being read. A 5-bullet hard cap forces prioritization and makes the report actually useful.

rule.statement: Constrain severity distribution: at most 20% High, 50% Medium, 30% Low. Agents default to Medium for everything — enforce the distribution via prompt constraint.
rule.rationale: Without distribution constraints, severity ratings are meaningless. An issue list where 90% of items are Medium provides no prioritization signal. The constrained distribution forces the evaluator to differentiate by frequency and task criticality.

rule.statement: High severity: Q2 or Q3 failure on a step required to complete the primary task, or Q4 failure that produces data loss. Medium severity: Q1/Q2/Q3 failures on secondary steps. Low severity: Q4 failures where success is still communicated, or steps on rarely-taken paths.
rule.rationale: Severity must correlate with user impact, not with how interesting the finding is. Tying severity to task criticality and data integrity gives stakeholders a defensible prioritization basis.

rule.statement: Track resolved, new, and regressed issues across each re-evaluation run. Report the delta, not just the absolute issue count. The delta is the only metric that proves the walkthrough is paying off.
rule.rationale: A report showing "12 issues found" every sprint provides no value. A report showing "8 resolved, 2 new, 1 regressed" demonstrates that the process is driving improvement and catches regressions before release.

rule.statement: When reconciling human and agent evaluators, weight human-only findings 1.5x in tie-breaks. Agent confidence language is uniformly high regardless of certainty — humans express genuine uncertainty.
rule.rationale: Agents produce consistently assertive language even when Q3 evidence is thin. Humans who rate a finding as No but hedge their confidence are expressing genuine uncertainty about edge cases. That hedging is signal, not noise.

rule.statement: Mark consensus findings (all evaluators agree) separately from disputed findings (evaluators disagree). Disputed findings require human review before being filed as tickets.
rule.rationale: Disputed findings often reflect ambiguous step definitions or evaluator expertise differences, not genuine Q1-Q4 failures. Auto-filing disputed findings as tickets creates noise that erodes developer trust in the walkthrough process.

section.name: report-structure
section.p: Executive summary: steps evaluated, issues found, critical issue count, key finding (1 sentence). Task overview: persona description, goal, optimal path step count. Findings by step: each step with issues, affected question (Q1-Q4), impact, recommendation. Issues summary table: step, issue, question, severity (H/M/L), status (open/fixed). Priority recommendations: Critical (fix before launch), Important (fix soon), Nice to have. Positive findings: at least 3 steps that worked — do not change these in future revisions.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Recommended subagents: walk-planner (converts spec → Walkthrough Planning Template with persona, task, action sequence, scope). walk-evaluator (per-step Q1-Q4 evaluator with vision; same as cog-walk-basics evaluator but bound to the locked step sequence). walk-reporter (aggregates evaluation JSON into the Summary Report Template; produces issue table with severity). walk-reconciler (merges N independent evaluators; marks consensus vs disputed findings). faion-sdd-executor-agent (wraps the whole pipeline as an SDD task with the report as the deliverable).

example.title: Planner Agent Prompt Pattern
example.code: Input: feature spec at <path>, target persona <persona.md>. Produce: filled Walkthrough Planning Template (markdown). Action sequence MUST be the minimum-clicks happy path. Number each step. Out of scope: any branch, error path, or expert shortcut.

example.title: Reporter Agent Prompt Pattern
example.code: Inputs: <step_*.eval.json> files, planning template. Produce: filled Summary Report Template. Rules: at most 3 High issues. Every issue cites step number + Q1/Q2/Q3/Q4 + one-line fix. Positive findings section: list at least 3 things that worked (anti-fixation).

rule.statement: Lock the action sequence before any evaluation runs. Mid-walk changes invalidate per-step JSON.
rule.rationale: Consistency across evaluators and across releases depends on a fixed action sequence.

rule.statement: Use 2-4 evaluators (mix of agent + human). Single-evaluator walks miss ~40% of issues per Nielsen heuristics literature.
rule.rationale: Multiple perspectives catch different failure classes and increase coverage.

rule.statement: Make agent evaluators independent: do not feed earlier step evaluations into later ones, or theme contamination explodes.
rule.rationale: Context drift causes agents to over-flag later steps based on earlier findings, inflating false Q2 failures.

rule.statement: Keep the report's executive summary to ≤5 bullets; force the agent into a token budget.
rule.rationale: Unconstrained reports become verbose; stakeholders skim them.

rule.statement: Track re-evaluation deltas (resolved / new / regressed) per release; this is the only metric that proves the walk is paying off.
rule.rationale: Absolute issue counts tell nothing; deltas show impact and catch regressions.

rule.statement: Tickets filed by agent should be labeled `auto-cog-walk` and reviewed in batch — do not auto-assign engineers.
rule.rationale: Agents over-flag minor issues; batch review catches false positives before they create alert fatigue.

antipattern.description: Action-sequence drift: agents like to "improve" the sequence mid-walk.
antipattern.reason: Pin it as immutable input in every prompt. Sequence changes invalidate all per-step comparisons across evaluators.

antipattern.description: Severity calibration is biased: agents over-mark Medium.
antipattern.reason: Constrain to a fixed distribution (e.g., max 20% High, 50% Medium, 30% Low).

antipattern.description: Q4 (progress) requires the post-action screen. Capture both before+after; static-only walks systematically miss missing-feedback issues.
antipattern.reason: Single screenshots cannot show UI state changes or presence/absence of feedback indicators.

template.purpose: Planning template: persona, task, correct action sequence table, interface link, scope
Per-step form: Q1-Q4 with Yes/No/Partial + notes, issues table with severity and suggestion
Walkthrough findings: executive summary, step issues, priority recommendations, positive findings
Shell pipeline driver: planner → Playwright captures → evaluators → reporter
